2.name: extent consume local food
2.content:
Today foods travel thousands of miles from the farm to the consumers. Some people say that it would be better for the economy and the environment if people ate food produced from local farmers. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Today, a considerable amount of food is transported over very long distances before it reaches consumers. Some people believe that eating food produced by local farmers would be better for both the economy and the environment. I largely agree with this view, although local production should only be encouraged where natural conditions allow.

To begin with, locally produced food can reduce the environmental damage caused by long and complex supply chains. Food that travels thousands of miles does not only require fuel for transportation; it passes through refrigerated warehouses, packaging facilities and multiple distribution centres before it is sold. Each stage consumes energy and raises the product's overall carbon footprint. Local food often avoids many of these processes entirely. Vegetables grown near a city, for example, can reach local markets within the same day, whereas imported produce may be chilled, wrapped and stored for weeks before consumers see it. Choosing local food therefore reduces not only transport emissions but the wider environmental burden of modern food logistics.

Local food consumption can also strengthen regional economies. When consumers buy from nearby farmers, money stays within the community rather than flowing to overseas suppliers or multinational companies. This gives small farmers a more stable income, supports local employment and helps rural areas remain economically active. If restaurants and households regularly source fruit, dairy and vegetables from local producers, those farmers can reinvest in better equipment, hire workers and expand their operations, improving the economic resilience of the whole region.

Admittedly, local food is not always the most sustainable choice. Some regions lack the climate or soil conditions needed to grow certain crops efficiently. Encouraging production in unsuitable areas may require excessive irrigation or fertilisers, raising costs and harming the environment. Local food should therefore be prioritised where it genuinely fits the region's natural capacity, not promoted indiscriminately.

In conclusion, eating locally produced food is generally beneficial for both the environment and the economy. However, local production should be encouraged selectively, particularly where it supports farmers without straining the region's natural resources.

2.name: Some people believe that students in schools learn
2.content:
Some people believe that students in schools learn better when they study in groups. Others say school students learn better when they study on their own. Discuss both these views and give your opinion.


The question of whether students learn better in groups or on their own is one that many people themselves have long debated. In this essay I will discuss both views before proving my opinion that both approaches carry genuine merit, and the best answer likely depends on the individual and the subject matter at hand.

Those who favor group study argue that learning is, at its core, a social experience. When students work together, they expose each other to different ways of understanding the same concept. A student who struggles with a topic might grasp it far more easily when a peer explains it in informal, relatable language rather than through a textbook. Group study also builds critical skills like communication, negotiation, and teamwork, all of which are essential beyond the classroom. There is also a motivational dimension to consider. Studying alongside others creates a sense of accountability. It is much harder to abandon a study session when classmates are counting on you to show up.

On the other hand, independent study has advantages that group settings simply cannot replicate. When a student studies alone, they learn at their own pace, free from the distractions, off-topic conversations, and social dynamics that group sessions often bring. Independent learners also develop stronger discipline and self-reliance, qualities that serve them well in examinations where no peer support is available. For students who are naturally introverted or who process information slowly and deeply, the quiet of solo study can be far more productive than the noise of a group.

In my view, neither approach is universally superior. The smarter position is a balanced one. Students should use group study to consolidate understanding, challenge their thinking, and prepare for collaborative tasks, but they should also invest time in independent revision to genuinely internalize what they have learned. Schools and teachers can play a meaningful role here by designing learning environments that consciously blend both methods rather than defaulting entirely to one. Ultimately, the most effective learner is one who knows when to collaborate and when to work alone.
